Table of contents

  • Data Processing
  • Item Evaluation
    • High missingness
    • Distributional Properties
    • Inter-item Correlations
      • Within sub-scales
      • Across sub-scales
    • Item-Total Correlations

Item Reduction

Author

Riccardo Volpato

Data Processing

library(tidyverse)
library(gtools)
library(psych)
library(faux)
library(knitr)

set.seed(8675309) # makes sure random numbers are reproducible
source("read_qualtrics_data.R")
d <- load_data("data/pilot/TPSai_17+March+2026_16.59.csv")
cols_tpsai <- d$cols_tpsai
# switch to d$items_data after data collection
items_data <- read_csv("data/pilot/simulated_item_data.csv") %>%
  select(all_of(cols_tpsai))  %>%
  # simulate columns with missingness
  faux::messy(prop = .4, "TR_01") %>%
  faux::messy(prop = .2, "DT_06") %>%
  # make a high correlation between DISTRUST_01 and _02
  mutate(DT_02 = DT_01 + sample(-1:1, n(), replace = TRUE),
         DT_02 = case_match(DT_02, 0 ~ 1, 6 ~ 5, .default = DT_02))

Item Evaluation

High missingness

High missingness items, where many people respond “Don’t know”, likely capture constructs that participants are uncertain about and can reduce the effective sample size and bias item statistics like means and correlations. Given that we have a substantial number of survey items, removing high missingness items is a way of shortening our survey by focusing on constructs that people have clearer ideas about.

We decided to drop items exceeding 30% missing responses. Our rationale is that if at least 3 out of 10 individuals were not able to meaningfully engage with an item, dropping it simplifies our scale and allows it to focus on measuring items with generally clearer responses. 30% is a relatively moderate threshold for removal of items with high missingness, which might avoid premature dropping of items with moderate missingness may still yield interpretable results.

However, beyond just dropping items with at least 30% missingness, we want to inspect the distribution of missing answers per item, grouped by sub-scale. When visually inspecting this distribution we will look for discontinuities, meaning items with a substantially higher proportion of “Don’t know” than other items in the same sub-scale. We may remove such items even if below our pre-set missingness threshold.

missing_threshold <- 0.3

missing_items <- items_data %>%
  summarise(
    across(all_of(cols_tpsai), ~ mean(is.na(.x)), .names = "{.col}_missing")
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to  = c("item", ".value"),
    names_sep = "_(?=[^_]+$)"
  ) %>%
  separate(item, c("scale", "q_n"), sep = "_", remove = FALSE)

ggplot(missing_items, aes(x = missing, fill = scale)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = missing_threshold)
missing_items %>%
  filter(missing > missing_threshold) %>%  # add in other criteria from visual inspection
  select(item, missing) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
item missing
TR_01 0.4

Distributional Properties

We examine the skewness and kurtosis of each item to assess whether their distributions deviate substantially from normality. Skewness captures the asymmetry of a distribution — whether responses cluster toward one end of the scale — while kurtosis captures the heaviness of the tails relative to a normal distribution. Both matter for factor analysis because standard maximum likelihood (ML) estimation assumes multivariate normality: when individual items depart strongly from normality, ML can produce biased parameter estimates, inflated chi-square statistics, and distorted standard errors (Kline 2016).

Following Kline (2016) , we treat absolute skewness and kurtosis values above 1.5 as indicative of meaningful non-normality. This is a moderate threshold, stricter than the ±1.0 sometimes recommended for smaller samples, but more conservative than the ±2.0 or ±3.0 thresholds occasionally used with larger ones. Items exceeding this threshold will be considered for removal alongside the other item reduction criteria. Where a substantial proportion of items show non-normality, we will additionally consider switching to a weighted least squares estimator (WLSMV) for the confirmatory factor analysis, as these approaches make no assumption of continuous normal distributions and are more appropriate for ordinal Likert data (Boateng et al. 2018).

dist_threshold <- 1.5

dist_data <- list(
  Trust    = d$cols_trust,
  Distrust = d$cols_distrust,
  Company  = d$cols_company
) %>%
  map_dfr(~ {
    items_data %>%
      select(all_of(.x)) %>%
      summarise(across(everything(), list(
        skew = ~ psych::skew(.x,    na.rm = TRUE),
        kurt = ~ psych::kurtosi(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
      ), .names = "{.col}__{.fn}")) %>%
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = c("item", "stat"), names_sep = "__") %>%
      pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = value)
  }, .id = "subscale")


ggplot(dist_data, aes(x = skew, y = kurt, colour = subscale, label = item)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-dist_threshold, dist_threshold), linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-dist_threshold, dist_threshold), linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Skewness", y = "Kurtosis")
dist_data %>%
  filter(abs(skew) > dist_threshold | abs(kurt) > dist_threshold) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
subscale item skew kurt

Inter-item Correlations

Next, we examine pairwise Polychoric inter-item correlations to identify redundant items. Polychoric correlations are used as they are more appropriate for ordinal Likert-scale data than Pearson correlations Boateng et al. (2018). Items correlating above .80 with another item are considered redundant, as this indicates the two items share more than 64% of their variance (r² = .64), leaving little unique contribution to the scale.

As per our missingness check, we will also visually inspect inter-item correlations for any discontinuity in distribution across sub-scales, potentially identifying items worth merging even if correlated under our pre-set threshold.

We examing itenr-item correlations within each TPSai sub-scale (Trust, Distrust, and AI Company) and across sub-scales.

Within sub-scales

ii_cor_threshold <- 0.3

ii_cor_data <- list(
  Trust = d$cols_trust,
  Distrust = d$cols_distrust,
  Company = d$cols_company
) %>%
  map_dfr(~ {
    items_data %>%
      select(all_of(.x)) %>%
      polychoric() %>%
      .$rho %>%
      as.data.frame() %>%
      rownames_to_column("item1") %>%
      pivot_longer(-item1, names_to = "item2", values_to = "r")
  }, .id = "subscale") %>%
  filter(r > -1 & r < 1) %>%
  filter(item1 < item2)
  
ii_cor_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = r, fill= subscale)) +
  facet_wrap(~subscale, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ii_cor_threshold) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -ii_cor_threshold) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 1, .2)) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1))
ii_cor_data %>%
  filter(abs(r) > ii_cor_threshold) %>%
  select(item1, item2, r) %>% # add in other criteria from visual inspection
  arrange(r) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
item1 item2 r

Across sub-scales

iia_cor_data <- 
  items_data %>%
  select(all_of(cols_tpsai)) %>%
  polychoric() %>%
  .$rho %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("item1") %>%
  pivot_longer(-item1, names_to = "item2", values_to = "r") %>%
  filter(r > -1 & r < 1) %>%
  filter(item1 < item2)  %>%
  # remove within sub-scale inter-item correlations
  filter(str_extract(item1, "^[A-Z]+") != str_extract(item2, "^[A-Z]+"))

iia_cor_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = r)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ii_cor_threshold) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -ii_cor_threshold) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 1, .2)) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1))
iia_cor_data %>%
  filter(abs(r) > ii_cor_threshold) %>%
  select(item1, item2, r) %>% # add in other criteria from visual inspection
  arrange(r) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
item1 item2 r

Item-Total Correlations

For each item, we compute the adjusted item-total correlation, the correlation between that item and the sum of all other items in the same sub-scale, excluding itself. Items with an adjusted item-total correlation below .30 are flagged for potential removal, as they contribute little to what the sub-scale is measuring overall (Boateng et al. 2018)

As with previous checks, we will also visually inspect the distribution of item-total correlations within each sub-scale, looking for discontinuities that may identify items worth removing even if they fall above our pre-set threshold. Particular attention will be paid to any negatively correlating items, which would suggest an item is moving in the opposite direction to the rest of its sub-scale — a strong signal of potential issues with wording or construct alignment.

it_cor_threshold <- 0.3

it_cor_data <- list(
  Trust    = d$cols_trust,
  Distrust = d$cols_distrust,
  Company  = d$cols_company
) %>%
  map_dfr(~ {
    items_data %>%
      select(all_of(.x)) %>%
      alpha() %>%
      .$item.stats %>%
      rownames_to_column("item") %>%
      select(item, r = r.drop)
  }, .id = "subscale")
Some items ( TR_03 TR_05 TR_06 TR_07 TR_09 TR_10 TR_19 ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option
Some items ( DT_06 DT_07 DT_13 DT_14 ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option
Some items ( CD_02 CD_07 ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option
it_cor_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = r, fill= subscale)) +
  facet_wrap(~subscale, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = it_cor_threshold) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -it_cor_threshold) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-1, 1, .2)) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-1, 1))
it_cor_data %>%
  filter(abs(r) > it_cor_threshold) %>%
  select(item, r) %>% # add in other criteria from visual inspection
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
item r
TR_01 -0.45
TR_02 0.65
TR_04 0.62

TR_08 0.52
TR_12 0.55
TR_13 0.67
TR_14 0.78
TR_15 0.82
TR_16 0.75
TR_18 0.79
TR_20 0.76
TR_22 0.58

DT_02 0.62
DT_04 0.50

DT_07 -0.41
DT_09 0.49
DT_10 0.57
DT_11 0.47
DT_13 -0.68
CD_01 0.76
CD_03 0.72
CD_04 0.67
CD_05 0.38
CD_06 0.57

References

Boateng, Godfred O., Torsten B. Neilands, Edward A. Frongillo, Hugo R. Melgar-Quiñonez, and Sera L. Young. 2018. “Best Practices for Developing and Validating Scales for Health, Social, and Behavioral Research: A Primer.” Frontiers in Public Health 6 (June). https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2018.00149.
Kline, Rex B. 2016. Principles and Practice of Structural Equation Modeling, 4th Ed. Principles and Practice of Structural Equation Modeling, 4th Ed. New York, NY, US: The Guilford Press.
